---
title: "US Data Center Siting Analysis"
subtitle: "A structural county-level model of data center location drivers"
author: "Georgetown University · 2025"
jupyter: python3
---

```{python}
#| echo: false
import warnings; warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import plotly.express as px
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from pathlib import Path

ROOT = Path("..").resolve()
ranking = pd.read_csv(ROOT / "data_revealed/04_tables/county_attractiveness_ranking.csv", index_col="rank")
prep    = pd.read_csv(ROOT / "data_revealed/04_tables/county_preprocessed.csv")
```

## Project Overview

This report presents a structural analysis of the county-level factors that drive data center
siting decisions across all 3,138 US counties. Rather than relying on ad-hoc heuristics,
we build a **two-part hurdle model** (LightGBM) that separately answers two questions:

1. *What makes a county likely to attract any data center at all?* (Stage 1 — Presence)
2. *Among counties with data centers, what drives the scale of that presence?* (Stage 2 — Scale)

This decomposition is methodologically important: the factors that determine *whether* a
data center exists often differ from those that determine *how many* exist in a given market.

::: {.callout-note}
**Live Dashboard**: An interactive version of the results is available at the
[Streamlit dashboard](https://data-center-siting-analysis.streamlit.app).
:::

## Key Numbers

```{python}
#| echo: false
total   = len(ranking)
with_dc = (ranking["num_datacenters"] > 0).sum()
pct     = with_dc / total * 100
top     = ranking.iloc[0]
zero_dc_high = ranking[(ranking["num_datacenters"] == 0) & (ranking["p_presence"] >= 0.5)]

summary = pd.DataFrame({
    "Metric": [
        "Counties in analysis",
        "Counties with ≥1 data center",
        "Zero-DC counties",
        "Structural features",
        "Stage 1 AUC (presence)",
        "Median absolute error (raw count)",
        "Top-ranked county",
    ],
    "Value": [
        f"{total:,}",
        f"{with_dc:,}  ({pct:.1f}%)",
        f"{total - with_dc:,}  ({100-pct:.1f}%)",
        "37",
        "0.847",
        "0.052",
        f"{top['county']}, {top['state']}  ({top['num_datacenters']:.0f} DCs)",
    ]
}).set_index("Metric")

summary
```

## Target Variable

Data center counts follow a highly zero-inflated distribution — 75.6% of US counties have
no data centers at all. This is the central modeling challenge that motivates the hurdle
approach over a simple regression.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "Distribution of data center counts across 3,138 US counties (log scale on y-axis)."
bins   = [0, 0.001, 1, 5, 10, 50, 100, 500]
labels = ["= 0", "(0,1)", "[1,5)", "[5,10)", "[10,50)", "[50,100)", "≥100"]
counts = pd.cut(ranking["num_datacenters"], bins=bins, labels=labels, right=False).value_counts(sort=False)

fig = px.bar(
    x=counts.index.astype(str), y=counts.values,
    labels={"x": "num_datacenters bucket", "y": "Number of counties"},
    color=counts.values, color_continuous_scale="Blues",
    text=counts.values,
)
fig.update_traces(textposition="outside")
fig.update_layout(
    showlegend=False, coloraxis_showscale=False,
    plot_bgcolor="white", paper_bgcolor="white",
    margin=dict(l=10, r=10, t=10, b=10), height=350,
    xaxis=dict(showgrid=False), yaxis=dict(showgrid=True, gridcolor="#eee"),
)
fig.show()
```

## Top Counties by Attractiveness Score

The model assigns each county an **attractiveness score** = P(presence) × E[scale | present].
This reflects both the probability of a county attracting any data center and the expected
intensity of that presence.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "Top 20 counties ranked by model attractiveness score."
top20 = ranking.head(20).copy()
top20["label"] = top20["county"] + ",  " + top20["state"]

fig = px.bar(
    top20, x="attractiveness", y="label",
    orientation="h", color="num_datacenters",
    color_continuous_scale="Plasma",
    labels={"label": "", "attractiveness": "Attractiveness Score", "num_datacenters": "Current DCs"},
    text="attractiveness",
)
fig.update_traces(texttemplate="%{text:.2f}", textposition="outside")
fig.update_layout(
    yaxis=dict(autorange="reversed"),
    plot_bgcolor="white", paper_bgcolor="white",
    margin=dict(l=10, r=80, t=10, b=10), height=520,
    coloraxis_colorbar=dict(title="Current DCs", thickness=12),
    xaxis=dict(showgrid=True, gridcolor="#eee"),
)
fig.show()
```

## Key Findings (Preview)

| Finding | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Power infrastructure dominates** | `clean_energy_jobs` and `grid_infrastructure_jobs` are the top two presence predictors — serving as proxies for grid capacity and power sector depth |
| **IT labor depth drives scale** | `wage_information` rises from rank 4 (presence) to rank 1 (scale), revealing an agglomeration effect among established markets |
| **Hazard indices are geographic confounders** | NRI risk scores appear in the top 8 presence predictors but drop to ranks 20–30 in scale — confirming they reflect where the existing market *is*, not causal attractors |
| **Electricity cost matters more at scale** | `commercial_price` rises from rank 9 to rank 4 between stages — cost sensitivity only becomes significant once a cluster exists |

See the [Interpretation](03-results.qmd) page for full SHAP analysis.
